items.text: No Vote, no census, as women do not count, they refuse to be counted, I have a conscientious objection to filling in this form.
items.context: Written in vivid blue ink across her 1911 census enumeration form as part of the Women's Freedom League mass census boycott, a nonviolent direct action to protest lack of voting rights for women.
items.publicReaction: The 1911 census boycott was a significant suffragette action covered in the press. Her participation as a prominent princess added high-profile attention to the Women's Freedom League's campaign.

items.text: He pushed a poor exhausted lady so that she fell onto her hands & knees & when she got up, he took hold of her most roughly and pushed her along.
items.context: Formal written complaint to the Secretary of State regarding police violence against suffragettes during the Black Friday protest of November 18, 1910, at Parliament Square. Sophia had witnessed a police officer's brutality and pursued him to obtain his identification number (V700).
items.publicReaction: Winston Churchill, the Home Secretary who received the complaint, responded with a curt note: 'Send no further reply to her.' The police defended their actions by claiming the women 'resorted to physical force,' dismissing the complaints as part of a discrediting effort.
items.source: Letter of complaint to Secretary of State, National Archives (HO 144/1106/200455)

items.text: The policeman was unnecessarily and brutally rough and Princess Sophia hopes he will be suitably punished.
items.context: Continuation of her formal complaint letter regarding police brutality witnessed during the Black Friday protest of 1910, specifically about the treatment of an exhausted woman suffragette.
items.publicReaction: The complaint was received by the Home Secretary but met with dismissal. The government refused to take action against the officer, with Churchill's response indicating the complaints would not be answered.
items.source: Letter of complaint to Secretary of State, National Archives (HO 144/1106/200455)

items.text: I am unable conscientiously to pay money to the state, as I am not allowed to exercise any control over its expenditure, neither am I allowed any voice in the choosing of members of Parliament, whose salaries I have to help to pay. This is very unjustified. When the women of England are enfranchised and the State acknowledges me as a citizen, I shall, of course, pay my share willingly towards its upkeep, if I am not a fit person for the purposes of representation, why should I be a fit person for taxation?
items.context: Statement made in court in December 1913 when summoned for refusing to pay licence fees for dogs, a carriage, and a servant. This was part of the Women's Tax Resistance League's campaign of 'No Vote, No Tax.' She had previously been fined £3 in May 1911 for similar violations.
items.publicReaction: The case generated significant publicity. The Daily Mail newspaper reported specifically on her trial. Following the court case, she was fined £12 10s and her possessions (a pearl necklace and gold bangles) were confiscated and auctioned at Twickenham Town Hall. Members of the Women's Tax Resistance League then purchased the items back at auction, making the case part of their public awareness strategy.
items.source: The Times (30th December, 1913); also cited in The Sikh Scholar, The National Archives

items.text: Oh, you wicked English, how I long for your downfall. How I loathe you all. … I am your deadly enemy from hereafter. Such injustice I cannot stand.
items.context: Written in her personal diary during her 1906-1907 visit to India, after she witnessed the arrest and sentencing to hard labour of nationalist activist Lala Lajpat Rai for sedition. This trip exposed her to Indian poverty and the reality of what her family had lost under British imperial rule.
items.publicReaction: Not publicly disclosed until after her death, when revealed through Anita Anand's biography. The diary entry marked a turning point in her political consciousness, leading her to become increasingly radical in her opposition to imperialism.
items.source: Sophia Duleep Singh's diary, National Archives; cited in Anita Anand's 'Sophia: Princess, Suffragette, Revolutionary'

items.text: I will come on the 9th to the meeting with pleasure. I hope you have found someone else to support the resolution, if not I will do so, but very much prefer not to and I shall only say about five words.
items.context: Letter to WSPU (Women's Social and Political Union) organizers expressing reluctance to speak publicly at meetings. Sophia had joined the WSPU in 1909 after returning from India, but was initially reluctant about public speaking.
items.publicReaction: Despite her stated preference to avoid speaking, she later became more active in addressing WSPU branch meetings, particularly at the Richmond WSPU branch, though she remained somewhat uncomfortable with large public speeches.
items.source: Referenced in articles about Sophia Duleep Singh; original source not specified in available sources

items.context: Entry in the 1934 edition of Women's Who's Who, where Sophia listed her life's interest and purpose.
items.publicReaction: This minimal but telling entry captured the essence of her lifelong activism and commitment to women's rights and suffrage, encapsulating her decades of work in a single phrase.

items.text: Dear Mr Montagu, the great rise in prices, taxes and rates, and the ever increasing difficulty of making two ends meet, leave me no choice but to write to you to ask for assistance.
items.context: Letter to the Secretary of State for India requesting financial assistance due to economic hardship and inability to cover living expenses.
items.publicReaction: Not widely publicized at the time; this letter represents her personal struggles after years of financial sacrifices made through tax resistance and financial support to suffragette causes.
items.source: Letter to Secretary of State for India

items.text: I have received your letter of October 18 with regard to the reduction of the allowance from £200 to £130.68 a year. As it was not sufficiently adequate in the first place to cover expenses, it will be much less so when reduced... I am again obliged to use capital, which will further reduce my income.
items.context: Letter expressing concern about a significant reduction in her annual allowance from Queen Victoria's estate, which was already insufficient to cover her living expenses.
items.publicReaction: Not widely publicized; the letter reflects her financial struggles in her later years as the value of her allowance diminished with inflation and economic changes following World War I.

items.text: I want a solemn promise from you. You are never, ever not to vote. You don't realise how far we've come.
items.context: Statement made near the end of her life, after World War II, to Drovna (her housekeeper's daughter), emphasizing the importance of exercising voting rights that women had fought so hard to obtain.
items.publicReaction: Recorded in Anita Anand's biography 'Sophia: Princess, Suffragette, Revolutionary' and represents her final public message about the value of women's suffrage rights.
items.source: Anita Anand's 'Sophia: Princess, Suffragette, Revolutionary'; also referenced in New Statesman article review
